elements.sent-2411-484: We used the same experimental setup as in Section~\ref{sec:eval-ac .. Model Training.}
elements.sent-2411-485: For this module, we trained the model using the following parameter settings: the minimum number of training flow statistics samples was 150, and the sampling rate was 5 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-486: We also set the maximum Euclidean distance threshold for anomaly detection to 0.8.
elements.sent-2411-487: Finally, we set the window size to 70 samples for UDP-based communications and 90 for TCP-based communications.
elements.figure-2411-013: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{rtfsl-udp-results-1.pdf}} \caption{ RTFSL module evaluation results for the UDP-based flows. \label{fig:rtfsl-udp-results-1} }
elements.sent-2411-488: We evaluate the efficacy of the RTFSL module in ensuring that access granted to endpoints is used in accordance with the learned transmission behavior from the training process.
elements.sent-2411-489: In this experimental setup, the ARL module initially grants communication access to endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-490: However, during the enforcement phase, the endpoints deviate from their normal behavior, either adopting the transmission patterns of another benign application or exhibiting malicious behavior.
elements.sent-2411-491: It is important to note that the model training and enforcement phases were conducted using different traffic trace datasets captured during different periods in the MAWI dataset.
elements.sent-2411-492: These capture periods experienced natural throughput fluctuations due to the starting and stopping of flows.
elements.sent-2411-493: As a result, during testing, the model attempts to match transmission patterns that may have varied due to these throughput fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-494: Therefore, we evaluate the performance of the RTFSL module in recognizing normality or abnormality in traffic patterns affected by the dynamic fluctuations of network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-495: Consistent with the evaluation objectives for the ARL module, we assess the RTFSL module's ability to (1) recognize normal benign transmission patterns and (2) detect deviations classified as abnormal benign or malicious behaviors.. Results.
elements.sent-2411-496: Figures \ref{fig:rtfsl-udp-results-1 and \ref{fig:rtfsl-udp-results-2 show the evaluation results for the UDP-based flows whereas Figures \ref{fig:rtfsl-tcp-results-1 and \ref{fig:rtfsl-tcp-results-2 show the evaluation results for the TCP-based flows.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-014: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{rtfsl-udp-results-2.pdf}} \caption{ RTFSL module evaluation results for the UDP-based flows (continued). \label{fig:rtfsl-udp-results-2} }
elements.sent-2411-497: Similar to the previous section, we present the results in a cross-evaluation manner where the vertical axis indicates the data on which the model is individually trained.
elements.sent-2411-498: For each training data, we report the number of training samples supplied to the model.
elements.sent-2411-499: The horizontal axis indicates the data on which the model is tested.
elements.sent-2411-500: Each cell in the evaluation results shows a plot of how the anomaly error (i.e., DTW Euclidean distance) progresses with the arrival of flow statistics samples for the packets (green curve) and bytes (orange curve).
elements.sent-2411-501: The dotted blue line denotes the moment when the sliding window starts (i.e., the number of collected samples equals the window length).
elements.sent-2411-502: The horizontal dotted line denotes the anomaly detection threshold (i.e., 0.8).
elements.sent-2411-503: The anomaly detection occurs when a curve meets the anomaly detection boundary.
elements.sent-2411-504: Note that if the green and orange curves are tangent to one another, only the orange curve will be visible in the plot, as it overlays the green curve.
elements.sent-2411-505: Plots in orange boxes pertain to normal benign tests, green to abnormal benign, and red to abnormal malicious.
elements.figure-2411-015: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1.1\textwidth]{rtfsl-tcp-results-1.png}} \caption{ RTFSL module evaluation results for the TCP-based flows. \label{fig:rtfsl-tcp-results-1} }
elements.sent-2411-506: In both types of flows, we make the following observations: (1) The DTW Euclidean distance for normal benign patterns typically remains far below the anomaly threshold.
elements.sent-2411-507: In other words, in all the cases, the module detected the abnormality by collecting statistics samples less than the window length (i.e., in the first couple of minutes of data transmission).
elements.sent-2411-508: For example, the model trained on the ``2024 Game 1 (UDP)'' application data and tested on ``2018 VoIP (UDP)'' detected the anomalous flow within 10 samples (less than a minute of data transmission) from the flow start (see Figure~\ref{fig:rtfsl-udp-results-1 ).}
elements.figure-2411-016: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1.1\textwidth]{rtfsl-tcp-results-2.png}} \caption{ RTFSL module evaluation results for the TCP-based flows (continued). \label{fig:rtfsl-tcp-results-2} }
elements.subsection-2411-008: RQ4: RTFSL Effectiveness in Changing Network Conditions
elements.figure-2411-017: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{rtfsl-reduced-tcp.png}} \caption{ RTFSL module evaluation results on different training and enforcement network conditions for the TCP-based flows. \label{fig:rtfsl-reduced-tcp-results} }
elements.sent-2411-509: We now explore a distinct scenario where the training data is generated under network conditions that differ from those present during model enforcement.
elements.sent-2411-510: This analysis aims to examine the module's robustness to variations in network conditions, specifically bandwidth, link delays, and jitter.
elements.sent-2411-511: It is worth noting that the experiments conducted with the MAWI datasets (Section~\ref{sec:rtfsl-effectiveness ) inherently include fluctuations in these network condition attributes over time due to the thousands of flows traversing the transit link from which the data was captured.}
elements.sent-2411-512: In this scenario, we intentionally introduce significant changes to these conditions to further stress-test the module's adaptability.. Experiment Setup.
elements.sent-2411-513: We use the experiment setup 2 discussed in Section~\ref{sec:eval-ac .}
elements.figure-2411-018: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{rtfsl-reduced-udp.png}} \caption{ RTFSL module evaluation results on different training and enforcement network conditions for the UDP-based flows. \label{fig:rtfsl-reduced-udp-results} }
elements.sent-2411-514: We train the module with data generated in a network with a bandwidth of 55 Mbps between the communicating entities and no observable link delays or jitter.
elements.sent-2411-515: In contrast, during model enforcement, we enforce the model in a network with significantly lower bandwidth (i.e., < 20 Mbps) and progressively introduce link delays and jitter.
elements.sent-2411-516: Our investigation focuses on two key aspects: (1) assessing whether normal benign transmissions are still accurately identified as benign, thereby preventing degradation of QoS by suspending benign flows; and (2) evaluating the model's ability to detect abnormal malicious flows under these changing conditions.. Results.
elements.sent-2411-517: The results of the experiments are presented in the Fugures \ref{fig:rtfsl-reduced-tcp-results and \ref{fig:rtfsl-reduced-udp-results .}}
elements.sent-2411-518: The name of each plot denotes the traffic patterns on which the module is tested as well as the network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-519: For instance, plot ``iperf B20\_D\_50\_J\_40,'' pertains to the exhibited transmission patterns of iperf when the bandwidth is 20 Mbps with link delays of 50 ms and jitter of 40 Ms. For both TCP- and UDP-based communication, the model demonstrates resilience against a reduction in bandwidth of at least 64\%.
elements.sent-2411-520: Specifically, the model exhibits no instances of FP or FN for either benign or anomalous malicious traffic under these conditions.
elements.sent-2411-521: Subsequently, we introduce a network delay of 50 ms along with a jitter of up to 60 Ms. In the case of TCP-based communication, the model identifies anomalies in the byte timeseries (orange curve), having an anomaly error of 0.8.
elements.sent-2411-523: It is noteworthy that network delay and jitter of such magnitude significantly diminish the QoS, even without ZT-SDN intervention.
elements.sent-2411-524: Typically, applications with high bandwidth requirements or real-time communications, such as Voice over IP, are unable to tolerate jitter beyond 50 ms~\cite{ciscoMerakiJitter, mediumJitter .}
elements.sent-2411-525: Thus, the communication, even without ZT-SDN, would be impractical in such scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-526: Conversely, in the case of UDP, the model remains robust even in the presence of extremely high delay and jitter.
elements.sent-2411-527: This resilience can be attributed to the simplicity of the UDP protocol, which lacks congestion control and retransmission mechanisms found in TCP.
elements.sent-2411-528: Consequently, the shape of the time series in these situations does not undergo notable changes that would trigger anomalies in ZT-SDN.
elements.sent-2411-529: Last, the abnormal malicious patterns exhibited by the flooding and port scanning are detectable in the reduced bandwidth setting.
elements.sent-2411-530: The model continues to exhibit robustness in detecting these attacks beyond 50 ms in both delay and jitter.
elements.sent-2411-531: We evaluate the performance of ZT-SDN by comparing it to the baseline provided by ONOS's reactive forwarding application, Fwd.
elements.sent-2411-532: Our evaluation focuses on three key metrics: (1) the number of control plane invocations (i.e., \verb|PACKET_IN| messages) from the data plane, (2) the achievable bandwidth in the data plane as a result of control plane processing, and (3) the ZT-SDN's control plane processing delay.
elements.sent-2411-533: Experiment Setup: We set up a network of varying sizes in terms of hosts, switches, and the number of switches between communicating entities.
elements.sent-2411-534: Both the endpoints and switches run individually in Linux containers within the Mininet environment.
elements.sent-2411-535: We utilize \verb|Open vSwitches|~\cite{pfaff2015design for the switches.}
elements.sent-2411-536: To generate traffic and measure the maximum achievable bandwidth between each client and server machine, we use the \verb|iperf| tool in bandwidth testing mode.
elements.sent-2411-537: In this mode, \verb|iperf| uses two bidirectional channels and sends packets rapidly to stress test the connection and measure the bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-538: In these experiments, we test the scalability of our approach under the most stressful network conditions: (1) all client machines start their applications approximately at the same time, and (2) the communication path between entities is always the longest possible, traversing all network switches.. Model Training.
elements.sent-2411-539: We train ARL and RTFSL similarly to Section~\ref{sec:eval-ac .}
elements.sent-2411-540: Using our RGAM approach, we mine rules from datasets generated by the CSM.
elements.sent-2411-541: The dataset comprises approximately 35,000 traffic samples from application execution.
elements.sent-2411-542: RGAM generated two flow rules for each communication endpoint: r1 = [dstAddr = $\mathit{SERVER\_ADDR}$, dstPort = 5001, dscp = 0, ecn = 0, etherType = 2048, proto = TCP, srcAddr = $\mathit{CLIENT\_ADDR}$, vlanID=-1], and r2 = [destAddr = $\mathit{CLIENT\_ADDR}$, dscp = 0, ecn = 0, etherType = 2048, proto = TCP, srcAddr = $\mathit{SERVER\_ADDR}$, srcPort = 5001, vlanID = -1], where $\mathit{SERVER\_ADDR}$ and $\mathit{CLIENT\_ADDR}$ are the IPv4 addresses the server and each client respectively.
elements.sent-2411-543: Here, $\mathit{SERVER\_ADDR}$ and $\mathit{CLIENT\_ADDR}$ represent the IPv4 addresses of the server and each client, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-544: According to our model analysis, the source port on any client machine cannot be predicted, and thus, it is excluded from the generated rules.
elements.sent-2411-545: Additionally, the analysis indicates that these two rules have a 100\% association score.. Results.
elements.sent-2411-546: Table~\ref{tab:rgam-results shows the scalability performance results comparing the Fwd with ZT-SDN.}
elements.sent-2411-547: The topology column reports the number of host machines (clients and server) and the number of switches in the path between each client and the server.
elements.sent-2411-548: Packet-In indicates the number of times the controller was invoked due to the absence of rules in the data plane.
elements.sent-2411-549: Number of rules expresses the number of rules installed at the switches by each approach.
elements.sent-2411-550: Note that Fwd generates rules dynamically at runtime based on \verb|PACKET_IN| message headers, which include source and destination addresses, ports, and transport layer protocol.
elements.sent-2411-551: Finally, the total processing time represents the time required for ZT-SDN and Fwd to process all the access requests (i.e., \verb|PACKET_IN|).
elements.sent-2411-552: For ZT-SDN, this includes the processing time of both the CSM and ML modules (encompassing packet headers processing and model inferences).
elements.sent-2411-553: It is important to note that Fwd does not enforce access control and allows all accesses.
elements.sent-2411-554: In all cases, ZT-SDN demonstrates a reduction in the number of \verb|PACKET_IN| messages compared to Fwd, owing to ZT-SDN's proactive rule deployment approach.
elements.sent-2411-555: Finally, the number of rules installed in the network is reduced by 50\% in ZT-SDN compared to rules installed by Fwd.
elements.sent-2411-556: This is because Fwd enforces rules with both the source and destination ports.
elements.sent-2411-557: Since, in this scenario, there are two bidirectional channels, the number of rules enforced by Fwd at each switch would be double the number of rules enforced by ZT-SDN.

elements.sent-2411-561: In this paper, we have introduced ZT-SDN, a novel end-to-end ZT architecture for SDN.
elements.sent-2411-562: ZT-SDN implements automated learning processes from the underlying network that (1) capture the communication requirements of entities in the network, (2) derive the access control rules allowing the entities to execute their missions successfully in the least privilege, and (3) learn the data transmission behavior of the entities using those granted network permissions by extracting their communication patterns.
elements.sent-2411-563: Additionally, ZT-Gym is introduced to facilitate the offline generation of application datasets and training of ML modules, ensuring benign training datasets in scenarios where network data transmissions are not guaranteed to be benign.
elements.sent-2411-564: Our experimental results demonstrate that ZT-SDN effectively prevents unauthorized access to network resources and detects deviant behaviors in entities using permitted flows.
elements.sent-2411-565: Furthermore, we show that ZT-SDN exhibits reasonable tolerance to changing network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-566: We also present the scalability performance of our framework across various topology sizes, comparing it against the ONOS baseline reactive forwarding.
